front: What is the definition of an incidence matrix for an undirected graph?
back: For an undirected graph with n vertices and m edges, the incidence matrix is an n x m matrix where the entry a_ij is 1 if vertex i is incident with (connected to) edge j, and 0 otherwise.

front: How is the incidence matrix defined for a directed graph?
back:
For a directed graph, the incidence matrix is an n x m matrix where the entry a_ij is:
• 1 if vertex i is the tail (starting point) of arc j
• -1 if vertex i is the head (ending point) of arc j
• 0 otherwise

front: What is one key property of the incidence matrix of an undirected graph?
back: The sum of entries in any column is always 2, because each edge is incident to exactly two vertices.

front: What does the sum of the entries in any row of an incidence matrix represent?
back: The sum of the entries in a row (for a given vertex) equals the degree of that vertex in an undirected graph. In a directed graph, the sum of the positive entries is the out-degree, and the sum of the absolute values of the negative entries is the in-degree.

front: What is the rank of the incidence matrix of a connected graph with n vertices?
back: The rank of the incidence matrix of a connected graph is n - 1.
